coredata.dc:title: Dual-source powered nanomotors coupled with dual-targeting ligands for efficient capture and detection of CTCs in whole blood and in vivo tumor imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Jiaoyu
Chen, Zekun
Ma, Enhui
Wang, Wenjun
Zheng, Shaohui
Wang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Circulating tumor cells (CTCs) are important biomarkers in cancer diagnosis. However, the specific labeling of CTCs with high capture efficiency in whole blood remains a problem. Herein, a dual-source-driven nanomotor coupled with dual-targeting ligands (CD@NM) was designed for efficient capture, specific imaging and quantitative detection of CTCs. In both water and biological fluid, CD@NMs moved autonomously under the propulsion of a magnetic field and H2O2 solution, which improved the capture efficiency of CTCs to 97.50 ± 2.38%. More importantly, specific labeling of CTCs was achieved by fluorescence quenching and recovery of fluorescent carbon dots modified on the CD@NMs. As a result, the CD@NMs exhibited efficient CTC capture, specific CTC imaging and recognition in whole blood. CD@NMs were also successfully deployed in the specific imaging of tumor tissues in vivo. On this basis, CD@NMs are expected to provide a new platform for tumor diagnosis both in vitro and in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanomotors
Dual-tumor targeting
Dual-modal propulsion

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20220161 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21905303 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2021QN1041, 2020ZDPY0213, 2022YCPY0205 ), and Promotion Fund for Youth Talent of Jiangsu Association for Science and Technology ( TJ-2021–069 ), the Open Fund of Xuzhou Medical University ( XYKF202102 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of γ-Fe2O3 nanorods, (b) TEM image of nanomotors, (c) UVvis absorption spectra, (d-e) TEM images and (f) XRD of carbon dots, (g-h) TEM images and (i) fluorescence spectroscopy of CD@NMs.

(a) The motion trajectories of CD@NMs in 10 mM H2O2, magnet, and 10 mM H2O2 + magnet over a period of 10 s (b) The motion trajectories of CD@NMs in water, PBS and FBS over a period of 10 s (c) The MSD of CD@NMs in 10 mM H2O2 (black line) and 10 mM H2O2 + magnet (blue line). (d) The velocity (black bar column), and diffusion coefficients (blue bar column) of CD@NMs in 10 mM H2O2 and 10 mM H2O2 + magnet. (e) The MSD of CD@NMs in water (blue line), PBS (pink line) and FBS (purple line) with 10 mM H2O2 + magnet. (f) The velocities (black bar column), and diffusion coefficients (bule bar column) of CD@NMs in water, PBS and FBS with 10 mM H2O2 + magnet.

Fluorescence microscopy images of 4T1 cells after incubation with CD@NMs at 10 mM H2O2 (a), magnet (b), and 10 mM H2O2 + magnet (c). (d) Capture efficiency of 4T1 cells after incubation with CD@NMs. (e) Optimizations of incubation time (e) and CD@NM concentrations (f).

Optical image (a) and fluorescence image (b) of L929 cells after incubation with CD@NMs. Optical image (c) and fluorescence image (d) of mixed cells after incubation with CD@NMs. (e) The linear equation related to the fluorescence intensity and the number of captured cells. (f) Verification of the linear equation.

Cell release experiment of 4T1 cells (a-b) and mixed cells (c-d). (e) The cell cytotoxicity of CD@NMs. The growth curve (f) and mRNA expression (g-h) of the released cells. The migration ability of normal 4T1 cells (i-j) and released 4T1 cells (k-l). Scale bars are 20 µm.
(a) Number of CTCs in the blood of various tumor-bearing mice. CD@NM-labeled and ICC-stained images of the captured CTCs in whole blood (b) and blood treated with red blood cell lysate (c).

(a) In vivo fluorescence images of mice at various time points after intravenous injection of CD@NMs. (b) The corresponding ex vivo fluorescence images of representative tissues at 12 h postinjection.
(a) Schematic fabrication process. (b) Capture, imaging and detection of CTCs based on CD@NMs.

head.ce:title: Dual-source powered nanomotors coupled with dual-targeting ligands for efficient capture and detection of CTCs in whole blood and in vivo tumor imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering & Technology, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 221116, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering & Technology, China University of Mining and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering & Technology, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, Jiangsu, 221116, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 221116, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu, 221116, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Circulating tumor cells (CTCs) are important biomarkers in cancer diagnosis. However, the specific labeling of CTCs with high capture efficiency in whole blood remains a problem. Herein, a dual-source-driven nanomotor coupled with dual-targeting ligands (CD@NM) was designed for efficient capture, specific imaging and quantitative detection of CTCs. In both water and biological fluid, CD@NMs moved autonomously under the propulsion of a magnetic field and H2O2 solution, which improved the capture efficiency of CTCs to 97.50 ± 2.38%. More importantly, specific labeling of CTCs was achieved by fluorescence quenching and recovery of fluorescent carbon dots modified on the CD@NMs. As a result, the CD@NMs exhibited efficient CTC capture, specific CTC imaging and recognition in whole blood. CD@NMs were also successfully deployed in the specific imaging of tumor tissues in vivo. On this basis, CD@NMs are expected to provide a new platform for tumor diagnosis both in vitro and in vivo.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has become a leading disease threatening human life and health [1]. Most cancer patients die not from the primary tumor but from the spread of cancer cells to other organs [2]. Cancer metastasis is a complex process. Cancer cells first break out of tumor tissue and invade into blood and lymph vessels, forming circulating tumor cells (CTCs). Then, CTCs colonize distant organs through the circulatory system in the process of dissemination. In other words, CTCs are one of the main causes of cancer metastasis [3]. Currently, CTCs are recognized as a reliable biomarker in cancer diagnosis [4–6]. Compared with traditional tissue biopsy, liquid biopsy used for CTC analysis is noninvasive and more acceptable to patients. However, various problems remain in CTC analysis, such as effective separation [7], controlled release [8] and reliable analysis [9].

Previously, a variety of methods have been developed to better isolate CTCs, such as magnetic nanomaterials [10,11] and targeted modification [12,13]. However, the isolation and counting of CTCs in whole blood remains a challenging task due to the extreme rarity of CTCs in blood and the interference caused by nontargeted blood cells. Therefore, increasing the odds of nanomaterials to contact and bind with CTCs is an effective way to improve the capture efficiency. Nanomotors are novel nanomaterials that can realize self-propulsion via chemical reactions or under external stimuli [14,15]. Autonomous propulsion of nanomotors improved the binding efficiency of materials to target analytes. The application of nanomotors in CTC analysis will increase the binding probability of nanomaterials to CTCs, resulting in more efficient CTC separations.

In addition, medical imaging has also been integrated into clinical practice to realize the precise and noninvasive diagnosis of cancer. As a traditional imaging method, fluorescence imaging has been widely applied in CTC analysis [16,17]. However, most fluorescence imaging methods suffer from a lack of specificity. Typically, fluorophores or fluorescent substances are attached to nanomaterials to image CTCs solely by the targeting properties of nanomaterials, whereas the fluorescence of untargeted or mistargeted nanomaterials affects the imaging and enumeration of CTCs [18,19]. Therefore, developing a new fluorescence imaging/counting method for CTCs and increasing the targeted delivery performance of nanomaterials are urgently needed for accurate cancer detection.

In this work, γ-Fe2O3 nanorods were used as the core to prepare nanomotors via layer-by-layer self-assembly technology (LbL). Folic acid (FA) and hyaluronic acid (HA) carbon dots as targeting agents were modified on the outer surface of the nanorods to obtain nanomotors (CD@NMs). The CD@NMs realized self-propulsion under the stimulation of an external magnetic field and hydrogen peroxide solution at a speed of 10.69 ± 1.79 µm/s. Moreover, CD@NMs could overcome the Brown force to achieve autonomous motion even in biological fluids at a speed of 6.35 ± 0.59 µm/s with an effective diffusion coefficient of 123.32 ± 9.43 µm2/s. Self-propelled movement and dual targeting capabilities enable the CD@NMs to capture more CTCs in less time. Under enzyme/magnet power, the cell capture efficiency of the CD@NMs was 97.50 ± 2.38%, while the capture efficiency was only 87.50 ± 3.88% without hydrogen peroxide stimulation. The fluorescence of hyaluronic acid carbon dots was quenched due to folic acid modification of the CD@NMs. When the CD@NMs targeted tumor cells, the receptors on the cell membrane bonded to folic acid and hyaluronic acid, which restored the fluorescence of hyaluronic acid carbon dots ( Scheme 1). Therefore, the “off-on” fluorescence switch of the CD@NMs enables their use for CTC imaging. In addition, a linear relationship was found between the fluorescence intensity and the number of captured CTCs. The number of CTCs in the sample could be estimated by using this linear equation. In animal blood CTC analysis, the errors of the captured cell number were approximately 0.22 ± 1.09 and 1.34 ± 2.38 for CD@NM staining in whole blood and the linear equation in treated blood, respectively. In addition, CD@NMs were used for in vivo tumor imaging in mice.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI), catalase (CAT), folic acid, hyaluronic acid, citric acid, fetal bovine serum (FBS), PBS buffer (pH=7.4), FeCl3·6 H2O and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were purchased from Beijing Innochem Co., Ltd. All other reagents were used without further purification.

Synthesis of γ-Fe2O3 nanorods [20]: FeCl3·6 H2O (0.32 g) and CTAB (0.5 g) were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water. The solution was first stirred for 30 min, transferred into a Teflon autoclave, and then reacted at 80 ℃ for 12 h. After that, the yellow product was centrifuged, washed with water, and dried in a freeze dryer. The product was finally heated to 550 ℃ and calcined for 8 h to obtain γ-Fe2O3 nanorods.

Synthesis of carbon dots: The carbon dots were synthesized according to previous reports with slight modification [21]. Hyaluronic acid (120 mg) and citric acid (20 mg) were first dissolved in 25 mL deionized water. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and maintained at 180 ℃ for 2 h. After the system was cooled to room temperature, the light-yellow product was purified with a dialysis bag for 12 h to obtain the carbon dots.

Synthesis of CD@NMs: The synthesis method of preparing CD@NMs was similar to our previous research [22]. First, PEI/CAT multilayers were wrapped on γ-Fe2O3 nanorods through layer-by-layer assembly technology. Meanwhile, the outermost PEI layer was replaced by a PEI-FA layer to obtain nanomotors. Then, the prepared carbon dot suspensions were added to the nanomotor solution and reacted for 1 h to obtain CD@NMs.
Characterization: Transmission scanning electron microscopy (TEM), UVvisible (UVvis) absorption spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD) and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to characterize CD@NMs.

The self-propulsion motion of CD@NMs under different propulsion modes (10 mM H2O2, magnet, and 10 mM H2O2 + magnet) was observed via an inverted microscope and Olympus cell Sens Dimension system. In addition, to investigate the impact of the medium on the self-propulsion performance of the CD@NMs, the motion behaviors of CD@NMs in different media (DI water, PBS buffer, 10% fetal bovine serum) were assessed.

Briefly, CD@NMs were added to the 4T1 cell suspension (media: 1 mL cell culture medium with 10% FBS, cell numbers: ∼100) and then incubated for 8 min under different sources (10 mM H2O2, magnet, and 10 mM H2O2 + magnet). After incubation, the captured cells were enriched using a magnet, and the cells that had not been adsorbed by the magnet were discarded. The number and images of the captured cells were measured by flow cytometry and fluorescence microscopy, respectively. In addition, the incubation time and the concentration of CD@NMs were optimized to improve the capture efficiency.

To determine the specialty of CD@NMs, the CD@NMs were added to the L929 cell suspension (cell numbers: ∼100) and mixed cell suspension of L929 cells (cell numbers: ∼1000) and 4T1 cells (cell numbers: ∼10) and then incubated for 8 min. The capture efficiency of L929 cells and the impact of the addition of L929 cells on the capture of 4T1 cells were observed. CD@NMs were added to the cell suspension with a series of 4T1 cell numbers (2−100). After incubation for 8 min, fluorescence spectroscopy of each sample was monitored. The linear equation was fitted between the cell numbers and the fluorescence intensity and verified through experiments.

To verify the release performance of CD@NMs, after magnetic cell enrichment, excessive folic acid was added and incubated for 1 h. Fluorescence images taken before and after FA treatment were used to observe cell release capabilities. To evaluate the impact of cell release behavior on cell activity, cell proliferation performance, cell gene expression and cell migration performance were characterized. In addition, the cytotoxicity of the CD@NMs was verified.

Fresh blood samples from 4T1 tumor-bearing female BALB/c nude mice were collected. In order to detect the circulating tumor cells in the blood, the whole blood of each tumor-bearing mouse was extracted from the eyeball. Then, the blood samples were incubated with CD@NMs for 8 min and measured in the same way as described above. Furthermore, CTC numbers were quantified according to the linear relation between cell numbers and the fluorescence intensity after blood samples were processed with RBC lysis buffer.

Tumor-bearing mice were injected intravenously with 100 μL CD@NMs (400 μg/mL). Twelve hours after injection, all mice were sacrificed. The fluorescent imaging performance of the main organs and tumors was measured to determine the targeted specificity of CD@NMs. To investigate the in vivo biosafety of the NM@hydrogel system, blood biochemistry and tissue histological analysis were performed. All experiments associated with animals were approved by the Animal Care Committee of Xuzhou Medical University and carried out in compliance with the guidelines.

In this study, γ-Fe2O3 nanorods were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The TEM image revealed the uniform nanorod morphology of the synthesized γ-Fe2O3 with high dispersity. As shown in Fig. 1(a), the width of the γ-Fe2O3 nanorods was approximately 35–55 nm, and the length was in the range of 250–350 nm. Next, γ-Fe2O3 nanorods were deployed as a template to adsorb PEI/CAT multilayers on the surface to prepare nanomotors through LbL assembly technology via static force (Fig. S1). The core-shell structures of the nanomotors can be clearly observed in Fig. 1(b). The γ-Fe2O3 nanorods adsorbed polymer multilayers with a thickness of 20 nm. After assembly, the width of the nanomotors increased to 80–100 nm, and the length range was approximately 350–450 nm. For better functionalization of the prepared nanomotors, we also synthesized HA carbon dots via a hydrothermal reaction. to graft onto their surface to prepare CD@NMs. The TEM image shown in Fig. 1(d) illustrates that the size of the carbon dots was approximately 3–5 nm. Furthermore, according to the high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 1(e), the interplanar spacing of the carbon dot was found to be approximately 0.21 nm. The XRD pattern of the carbon dots shown in Fig. 1(f) displayed a diffraction peak at 28.84°, corresponding to the (100) plane, which was well matched with the high-resolution TEM image [23]. After modification with carbon dots, the surface of the CD@NM becomes rough (Fig. 1(g-h)). Meanwhile, UVvis absorption spectra were used to verify the successful synthesis of CD@NMs. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the UVvis absorption spectrum of CD@NM displayed an absorption peak at 217 nm, which corresponds to PEI; an absorption peak at 284 nm, which proves that carbon dots were adsorbed; and an absorption peak at 376 nm, which represents FA. In addition, the adsorbed carbon dots endow the function of the fluorescent indicator to the CD@NM. The integrated carbon dots can emit fluorescence (emission wavelength at 440 nm) at an excitation wavelength of 354 nm. When carbon dots were grafted onto the surface of the nanomotor, an efficient inner filter effect between the carbon dots and FA resulted in fluorescence quenching [24]. However, when the FA on the CD@NMs is combined with the folic acid receptor on the cell membrane, the fluorescence of the carbon dot will be restored (Fig. 1(i)), so that the CD@NM can act as a fluorescent indicator. The stability of the fluorescence of CD@NMs in different solutions (water, PBS, FBS) at different times was tested. As shown in Fig. S2, there was no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of CD@NMs in different solutions within 12 h, which confirmed the accuracy of CTC detection.

The CD@NMs with γ-Fe2O3 nanorods as the core could be actuated independently under the power of a magnetic field [25,26]. In addition, CAT, in which the CD@NMs are adsorbed on the surface, and the asymmetric structure of the CD@NMs can generate a concentration gradient through the degradation of hydrogen peroxide, thereby promoting nanomotor motion [27,28]. The autonomous motion of CD@NMs under different propulsion modes (10 mM H2O2, magnetic field, and 10 mM H2O2 + magnetic field) was investigated. The motion trajectories, mean square displacement (MSD), velocity, and diffusion coefficients were measured. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the motion trajectory of CD@NM in 10 mM H2O2 is irregular when the motion trajectory in the magnetic field is a straight line toward the magnetic field. Under the combined treatments of both magnetic fields and hydrogen peroxide, the motion trajectory of CD@NM is generally a straight line toward the magnetic field, with slight fluctuations. The curved MSD-Δt plot shown in Fig. 2(c) is a parabolic line at short time scales, and the curve becomes a straight line with a large slope with longer time scales. These results indicate that the motion of the CD@NM is a ballistic motion at a limited time scale, and the motion changes to diffusive behavior with a greatly enhanced diffusion coefficient at a long time scale [29]. The change from ballistic to diffusive behavior proved the self-propulsion of the CD@NMs. After the software processed the motion trajectories, the velocity and the effective diffusion coefficients of the CD@NMs were calculated. The average velocity of CD@NMs is 5.51 ± 1.43 µm/s at 10 mM H2O2, 5.29 ± 1.23 µm/s at the magnet, and 10.69 ± 1.79 µm/s at 10 mM H2O2 + magnet. The effective diffusion coefficients of CD@NMs are 8.61 ± 1.13 µm2/s at 10 mM H2O2, 91.45 ± 5.44 µm2/s at the magnet, and 159.50 ± 12.25 µm2/s at 10 mM H2O2 + magnet. In addition to the motion study in a pure water environment, the motion performance of the CD@NM motor in biological fluids (PBS, 10% FBS) was also investigated (Video S1). As shown in Fig. 2(f), the velocities and the effective diffusion coefficients of the CD@NMs in PBS and 10% FBS are reduced compared to those in water. The average velocity of CD@NMs was 8.65 ± 0.73 µm/s in PBS and 6.35 ± 0.59 µm/s in 10% FBS. The effective diffusion coefficients of CD@NMs in PBS and 10% FBS are 236.78 ± 6.05 µm2/s and 123.32 ± 9.43 µm2/s at 10 mM H2O2 + magnet, respectively. The results proved that the CD@NMs in biological fluids could still overcome Brownian forces to achieve self-propulsion (Fig. 2(e)).
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2023.113568.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
Motion performance of the CD@NM motor in water, PBS, and FBS.
.

The self-propulsion performance and dual-targeting capability of CD@NMs was modified with FA and HA to improve their efficiency in targeting tumor cells. To verify the specificity and sensitivity of the CD@NMs, in vitro cell capture experiments were performed. In this work, 4T1 cells were selected as the tumor cell model. To achieve higher tumor cell capture efficiency, the concentration of CD@NMs and incubation time were optimized. As shown in Fig. 3(e), with increasing incubation time (2 min, 4 min, 6 min, 8 min and 10 min), the cell capture efficiency also increased by 54.75 ± 3.36%, 68.76 ± 4.17%, 73.00 ± 4.33%, 97.50 ± 2.38% and 97.00 ± 2.85%, respectively. Considering the requirements of fast capture, a high capture efficiency must be obtained in a limited incubation time. Therefore, the incubation time was determined to be 8 min according to the previous test. On the other hand, as the concentration of the motor increased (100 μg/mL, 200 μg/mL, 400 μg/mL, 600 μg/mL and 800 μg/mL), the efficiency of tumor cell capture first increased and then decreased (40.88 ± 2.39%, 68.13 ± 3.98%, 97.50 ± 2.38%, 91.25 ± 5.32% and 81.75 ± 4.77%) (Fig. 3(f)). It is considered that the increase in the concentration of CD@NMs at the beginning can increase the chance of contact with tumor cells. However, as the concentration of the CD@NMs continues to increase, the tumor cell targets tend to saturate. On the other hand, a high concentration of CD@NMs will make CD@NMs aggregate and affect their function, resulting in reduced capture efficiency [30]. As a result, the capture efficiency would be reduced. Therefore, the concentration of CD@NMs for cell capture was set as 400 μg/mL. The capture efficiency of the CD@NMs under different external conditions (10 mM H2O2, magnet, and 10 mM H2O2 + magnet) was explored when the concentration of CD@NMs was 400 μg/mL and the incubation time was 8 min. As shown in the results (Fig. 3(a-c)), more fluorescent CD@NMs indicated more effective capture of cancer cells, as the fluorescence of carbon dots would be recovered upon attachment to cancer cells. The capture efficiency of CD@NMs is 59.67 ± 3.37% at 10 mM H2O2, 87.50 ± 3.88% at the magnet, and 97.50 ± 2.38% at 10 mM H2O2 + magnet (Fig. 3(d)). The CD@NMs can propel autonomously in 10 mM H2O2, which increases the chance of contact with tumor cells so that more tumor cells are captured. The magnet can adsorb the CD@NMs, thereby promoting the enrichment of the cells captured by the CD@NMs. Therefore, the dual effects of hydrogen peroxide and magnets improved the capture efficiency of the CD@NMs.

In addition, L929 cells were used as a normal cell model. Then, 400 μg/mL CD@NMs were incubated with the L929 cells (media: cell culture medium with 10% FBS, volume: 1 mL, cell numbers: ∼100) for 8 min. After incubation, the L929 cells were observed under a microscope directly, without the enrichment step of magnets. Compared with the optical image and the fluorescence image ( Fig. 4(a-b)), it can be found that L929 cells are not captured and marked by CD@NMs. The low expression of folic acid receptor and hyaluronic acid receptor on the cell membrane of normal cells made it unfavorable for capture by CD@NMs. Meanwhile, the CD@NMs were added to the mixed cell suspension (L929 cells: 4T1 cells = 100:1, media: cell culture medium with 10% FBS) and then incubated for 8 min. As shown in Fig. 4(c-d), the CD@NMs can still accurately capture 4T1 cells in the mixture of L929 cells and 4T1 cells, which proved that the existence of a large number of normal cells will not affect the ability of the CD@NMs to capture and mark tumor cells. The folic acid on the CD@NMs quenched the fluorescence of hyaluronic acid carbon dots. However, when folic acid and hyaluronic acid were combined with corresponding receptors, the fluorescence of carbon dots was restored. Therefore, the CD@NMs could mark tumor cells with fluorescence signals. According to the principle, the fluorescent intensity should be correlated to the number of captured cells. To verify this hypothesis, CD@NMs were incubated with cell culture medium containing different quantities of tumor cells. After incubation, the fluorescence intensity of the cell culture medium was measured. The plot of the fluorescent intensities against the number of tumor cells showed that the fluorescent intensity of the cell medium was linearly related to the number of captured cells, and the linear equation was y = 4.32 + 0.76x (R2 =99.61%) (Fig. 4(e)). To verify the reliability of the linear equation, L929 cells (cell numbers: ∼1000) were mixed with different numbers of 4T1 cells. After mixing, the cells were incubated with the CD@NMs to detect the fluorescence intensity. Flow cytometry was used to measure the number of captured cells, and the results were compared with the number of cells calculated by the linear equation. As shown in Fig. 4(f), compared with the actual amount, the error of the captured cell number calculated by the linear equation is approximately 1.26 ± 1.37. The results proved that the number of captured tumor cells could be accurately estimated according to the linear equation.

The cells incubated with CD@NMs were co-incubated with excess folic acid for 30 min for FA receptor blocking. Excess folic acid binds to folic acid receptors on cell membranes, which causes the CD@NMs to be competitively released from cells [31]. As shown in the results, most of the captured 4T1 cells were released, either with only captured cells ( Fig. 5(a-b)) or with L929 cells mixed in (Fig. 5(c-d)). Whether the released cells exhibited good proliferation performance was crucial for subsequent analysis of the circulating tumor cells. The released 4T1 cells were collected, and the density of the single-cell suspension was adjusted to 105 cells/mL. Cells were seeded in 24-well plates at 1 × 104 cells per well, and 5 parallel samples were set in each group. The cells were incubated at 37 ℃ in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Starting from the 24th hour, a 24-well plate was randomly selected every 24 h. The cells in the plate were counted, and the average value was calculated. Taking the culture time as the horizontal axis and the cell number as the vertical axis, the growth curve of the cells was depicted. According to Fig. 5(f), the trend of the growth curve of the released cells is consistent with that of the normal 4T1 cells, indicating that the released cells maintained good proliferation performance. Meanwhile, the migration ability of released 4T1 cells was investigated through a wound healing assay. The scratching gap of the control group disappeared completely after 24 h of incubation (Fig. 5(i-j)). The scratched gap of released 4T1 cells is healed, but traces remain (Fig. 5(k-l)), which illustrates that the released 4T1 cells still have good migration ability, but the migration ability is weakened compared with that of normal 4T1 cells. Furthermore, qPCR was used to determine whether the protein of the released cells was expressed normally. Multidrug resistance-associated protein (MRP-1) and multidrug resistance protein (MDR) are important proteins in tumor cells [32,33]. The mRNA levels of MRP-1 and MDR were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5(g-h), the extent of MRP-1 and MDR gene expression in the released 4T1 cells was 0.97 ± 0.08 and 0.98 ± 0.05, while the extent of gene expression in the blank group was 1.00 ± 0.04 and 1.00 ± 0.13, respectively. Meanwhile, qPCR experiments illustrated that the released 4T1 cells could be used for subsequent gene analysis. All the above results demonstrated that capture and release behavior did not affect the performance of circulating tumor cells. Moreover, a CCK-8 assay was employed to investigate the cell cytotoxicity of CD@NMs. Different concentrations (100–800 µg/mL) of CD@NMs were incubated with L929 cells. After 24 h, 48 h and 72 h of incubation, CCK8 solution was added and incubated for another 1.5 h. Subsequently, the absorbance of the cells at 450 nm was assessed by a microplate reader. As shown in Fig. 5(e), the viability of cells remained at 91.51 ± 1.74% at 72 h with 400 µg/mL CD@NMs. Even when the concentration of CD@NMs reached 800 µg/mL, the cell viability was still higher than 85% (86.36 ± 0.92%). The result of the CCK-8 assay illustrates the low toxicity of CD@NMs.

In view of the excellent ability of the CD@NMs to capture CTCs in vitro, the clinical application of CD@NMs was further verified by collecting blood samples from 9 tumor-bearing mice. Then, the number of CTCs per mL of blood was measured via CD@NM fluorescence staining and fluorescence spectra. It should be noted that when using the CD@NMs for fluorescence imaging, the blood samples did not need to be processed, and whole blood could be directly used. However, the fluorescence spectra must be measured after the red blood cell lysate is used to treat blood samples. Fig. 6(b) shows images of immunofluorescence staining and CD@NM labeling of captured CTCs in whole blood samples. It was found that the coincidence between ICC and CD@NMs is good. Although the effect of fluorescence staining is better after removing the red cells in the blood sample (Fig. 6(b-c)), the CD@NMs can dye the whole blood, which simplifies the staining steps and reduces the detection time. In addition, the number of captured CTCs in blood samples of ICC, CD@NMs fluorescence staining and the linear relationship in the fluorescence spectrum were compared. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the error of the captured cell number is approximately 0.22 ± 1.09 and 1.34 ± 2.38 for CD@NM staining and the linear equation, respectively. These results prove that CD@NMs have great application prospects in the clinical detection of CTCs.

After proving efficient CTC capture and imaging by CD@NMs in vitro, the specific imaging ability of tumor tissues in vivo was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 7(a), CD@NMs gradually accumulated in the tumor tissue and revealed fluorescence responsively to achieve fluorescence imaging after intravenous injection via the tail vein. The relative fluorescence reached a maximum peak at 60 min postinjection and then faded over time. In contrast, no fluorescence signal was observed in other major organs, which indicated the excellent target imaging performance of CD@NMs. In addition to the inevitable accumulation of CD@NMs in the liver, there was no obvious fluorescence signal in other dissected tissues at 12 h postinjection (Fig. 7(b)), which further demonstrated the specific imaging in vivo of CD@NMs.

In summary, we designed a stimuli-responsive, double-targeted and dual-mode-driven nanomotor for specific CTC capture, imaging and quantitative detection. Due to the specific binding of FA and HA modified on CD@NMs with the overexpressed folate receptor on tumor cells and the self-propulsion of CD@NMs under the drive of a magnetic field and H2O2, CD@NMs can capture CTCs efficiently and sensitively. Owing to the “off-on” fluorescence switch resulting from the regulated interactions between the FA quencher and the fluorescent HA carbon dots, CD@NMs can quantify the CTCs in whole blood with errors of 0.22 ± 1.09 and 1.34 ± 2.38 for CD@NM staining and the linear equation, respectively. Moreover, it has been proven that CD@NMs can be used for fast targeted imaging of tumor tissues in vivo. The synergistic integration of efficient CTC capture, imaging and accurate tumor-targeted imaging into a platform offers new prospects for tumor diagnosis in vitro and in vivo.

Jiaoyu Ren: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Zekun Chen: Validation, Investigation. Enhui Ma: Data curation, Visualization. Wenjun Wang: Investigation. Shaohui Zheng: Methodology, Resources, Supervision. Hong Wang: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
